schema_promotion_candidates.description: London penetration depth λ of the superconducting film in microns, extracted from fitting sheet kinetic inductance vs film thickness data to Lk/□(t) = µ0λ coth(t/λ)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Lambda determines sheet kinetic inductance at all thicknesses; beta-Ta has λ = 1.78 µm (~10x larger than alpha-Ta), which shifts resonance frequencies significantly and must be accounted for in device design — tracking this across films enables reliable frequency prediction and connects to mean free path and dirty/clean limit classification
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 4-5; Figure 2c: λ = (1.78 ± 0.02) µm

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Sheet kinetic inductance Lk/□ in pH/□, the kinetic inductance per square of the superconducting film, extracted from resonator frequency measurements
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Lk/□ determines resonator frequency shifts and kinetic inductance fraction α; for beta-Ta it ranges from ~3 to ~260 pH/□ depending on thickness — systematic tracking enables resonator and qubit design across thickness space and quantifies a distinctive material property of high-lambda superconductors
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 4-5; Figure 2c; Table S4

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Effective kinetic inductance fraction α = 1 - (fr,measured/fr,simulated)^2, dimensionless (0 to 1), measured per resonator
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Alpha directly quantifies how much the resonance frequency is shifted by kinetic inductance; for beta-Ta CPW resonators alpha ranges from 0.18 to 0.98 — this is a design-critical parameter that must be compensated in qubit wiring layout and is a direct signature of the material's kinetic inductance
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 4; Figure 2b; Table S4

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Average grain size of the superconducting film surface measured by AFM, in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Grain size directly determines grain boundary density and effective oxide volume at the MA interface — beta-Ta has ~10 nm grains vs 50-200 nm for alpha-Ta, and authors cite this as a contributor to higher surface TLS loss; systematic tracking would enable correlation between grain size and tan_delta across material platforms
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Supporting Information Section S9: 'grain sizes of approximately 10 nm'; Figure S10e

catchall.free_notes: This record captures the film characterization data for the beta-Ta films used in this study. The Hall bar measurement was performed on a film of thickness 263 nm (same as several resonator films). The normal-state resistivity value of 180 µΩ·cm is ~4-180x larger than alpha-Ta (1-50 µΩ·cm), consistent with the beta phase. RRR of 1.02 indicates nearly temperature-independent resistivity in normal state, consistent with high disorder.

additional_measurements.description: Magnetic penetration depth lambda extracted from Lk/□ vs thickness fit
additional_measurements.value: λ = (1.78 ± 0.02) µm; ~10x larger than alpha-Ta; consistent with BCS dirty limit estimate of λ_dirty ≈ 1.7 µm
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 4-5; Figure 2c; Supporting Information Section S7
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Large lambda (~µm scale) confirms beta-Ta is in BCS dirty limit (mean free path l < 1 nm from resistivity estimate); distinguishes vortex loss physics from clean-limit alpha-Ta

additional_measurements.description: CPW resonator (F1-CPW-8) with highest reported Lk/□ alongside high Qint: Lk/□ = (259 ± 33) pH/□ and Qint = (1.6 ± 0.1) × 10^6 at -90 dBm, 10 mK
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 5; Supporting Information Figure S6a

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Highest single-photon Qi measured in this study from resonators; lumped-element geometry with low pMS achieves high Qi

additional_measurements.source: Supporting Information Section S5, Figure S7c

correlations_observed.nature: Inverse linear relationship; tan delta = (1.6 ± 0.1) × 10^-3 describes all data; no film-thickness dependence of tan delta observed

correlations_observed.nature: Alpha increases with decreasing s and decreasing t

fabrication_details.description: Photolithography: HMDS vapor priming at 148°C in YES Vacuum Oven; AZ 1518 spun at 4000 rpm, 1000 rpm/s ramp, 40 s, baked 95°C 1 min → 1.9 µm resist. Exposure: Heidelberg DWL66+, 10 mm write head, 405 nm, 25% attenuation, 70% intensity, optical autofocus -10% focus offset. Post-exposure bake: 110°C 2 min. Development: AZ 300 MIF 90 s, DI water rinse 30 s, N2 dry.

Resonator chips packaged by wire-bonding to PCB; chip carrier: Quantum Machines QCage.24 (part numbers Q401, Q402-5, Q403 Al, Q404 Al); wrapped in aluminized Mylar for IR shielding; mounted on mixing chamber stage; enclosed by magnetic shield QCage Q428; outer magnetic shield on mixing chamber stage
Dilution refrigerators: Bluefors XLD400 or LD400; base temperature 10-15 mK

Resonator transmission measurements: Streamline P5008A VNA; probe power varied with Vaunix LDA-608V-4 programmable attenuator; output amplified by RF-Lambda RLNA02G08G30; temperature stabilized to within 1% for ≥1 hour before measurements
Finite element simulations for pMS: Ansys Maxwell; MA, MS, SA interfaces modeled as 3 nm-thick dielectric layers with ϵr = 10
EM simulations for resonator geometry: Cadence AWR AXIEM planar method-of-moments solver; sapphire modeled as uniaxially anisotropic dielectric ϵx=ϵy=9.3, ϵz=11.5

schema_promotion_candidates.description: For lumped-element resonators: the gap (spacing) between capacitor pads in microns, equivalent to the geometric parameter controlling pMS
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: LE resonator pMS is controlled by pad spacing (reported as s in their notation: 5, 15, 35, 65, 100 µm); this is geometrically distinct from CPW gap width and requires a separate field to enable participation ratio calculations for LE resonators
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Supporting Information Table S4: LE resonators labeled with s values 5, 15, 35, 65, 100 µm representing pad spacing

additional_measurements.source: Supporting Information Section S10; Table S5
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qother is much lower for beta-Ta-on-Si than for beta-Ta-on-sapphire, indicating additional non-TLS loss at the beta-Ta/Si MS interface; limits achievable Qint and would cap qubit T1 if used for qubit fabrication

additional_measurements.description: QTLS,0 for Si substrate resonators from Table S5
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: QTLS,0 is lower for beta-Ta on Si than on sapphire, suggesting additional surface TLS loss at beta-Ta/Si interface or from non-saturable MS loss

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Amorphous interfacial layer at MS interface is a candidate TLS source; thinner than beta-Ta/sapphire interface (5 nm) but loss mechanism may differ; authors cite MS interface as plausible candidate for non-saturable loss

additional_measurements.description: Unidentified XRD peak at 46.5° not matching beta-Ta or Si references

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear — may indicate a minority secondary phase or interfacial compound; authors do not assign it

anomalous_observations.description: Beta-Ta on silicon shows power- and temperature-independent losses (Qother < 3 million) that are absent or much weaker in beta-Ta on sapphire; the loss is independent of resonator geometry (pMS), suggesting a non-TLS, non-QP origin
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: non-saturable dielectric loss originating from the beta-Ta/Si MS interface, whose structure differs from the alpha-Ta/Si interface (which is lattice-matched). Authors note this requires further investigation.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The power- and temperature-independent internal quality factor Qother extracted from fitting Qint(n,T) to the TLS+QP+other loss model; represents the high-power asymptote of Qint
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Qother captures all non-TLS, non-QP loss channels and sets a ceiling on achievable Qi at high powers; in beta-Ta-on-Si resonators Qother < 3e6 (vs >10^6 for beta-Ta-on-sapphire), making it the dominant limiter — tracking this separately from QTLS_0 is essential for loss budget attribution
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Supporting Information Table S4 and S5; Section S8

beta-Ta-film-characterization.profile_notes: RRR of 1.02 and resistivity of 180 µΩ·cm confirm dirty limit; no microwave device measured so loss_mechanisms and coherence_tier are not applicable. Crystal phase and surface oxide characterization are explicitly linked to TLS loss in the record.

beta-Ta-resonators-sapphire-tan-delta.profile_notes: Qi up to 4.8×10^6 (LE resonator) qualifies as state_of_the_art; dominant loss is surface TLS at MS and MA interfaces confirmed by linear pMS scaling across 71 resonators. Dirty limit confirmed by lambda ~1.78 µm consistent with BCS dirty limit estimate.

qubit-1.profile_notes: T1 = 397 µs exceeds 100 µs threshold for state_of_the_art; OCS transmon regime (EJ/EC=16) introduces charge noise as an identified dephasing mechanism. Beta-Ta dirty limit established from film characterization sample.

qubit-2.profile_notes: Best qubit in study with T1 = 585 µs (max single-shot 958 µs); state_of_the_art tier. OCS transmon regime (EJ/EC=19) confirmed charge noise contribution to dephasing.

qubit-3.profile_notes: T1 = 400 µs is state_of_the_art; OCS transmon (EJ/EC=20) with charge noise contribution to dephasing inferred from regime.

qubit-4.profile_notes: T1 = 423 µs is state_of_the_art; OCS transmon (EJ/EC=22) with charge noise contribution to dephasing.

qubit-5.profile_notes: T1 = 329 µs is state_of_the_art; EJ/EC=28 places this at the boundary of the OCS regime, with charge noise still a contributing dephasing mechanism.

qubit-6.profile_notes: T1 = 236 µs is state_of_the_art; non-OCS transmon (EJ/EC=74) with T2,E > T1 indicating lifetime-limited coherence and negligible charge noise. Radiation loss included because authors hypothesize frequency-dependent coupling to spurious package modes at higher frequencies.

qubit-7.profile_notes: T1 = 148 µs is state_of_the_art; non-OCS transmon (EJ/EC=77) with T2,E > T1 confirming lifetime-limited coherence. Radiation included per authors' hypothesis on frequency-dependent package loss.

qubit-8.profile_notes: T1 = 97 µs falls in the competitive tier (10–100 µs); non-OCS transmon (EJ/EC=82). Radiation loss hypothesized by authors for higher-frequency qubits in this set.

qubit-9.profile_notes: T1 = 111 µs just crosses the state_of_the_art threshold; non-OCS transmon (EJ/EC=99). Radiation loss hypothesized for higher-frequency qubits.

qubit-10.profile_notes: T1 = 137 µs is state_of_the_art; non-OCS transmon (EJ/EC=98). Radiation loss hypothesized by authors for higher-frequency qubits.

qubit-11.profile_notes: T1 = 40 µs places this in the competitive tier; lowest Q in the study despite deepest non-OCS regime. Authors explicitly hypothesize frequency-dependent radiative coupling to package spurious modes as the dominant loss mechanism for this highest-frequency qubit.

beta-Ta-on-silicon-resonators.profile_notes: Qi up to 3×10^6 qualifies as state_of_the_art for resonators; non-TLS power- and temperature-independent loss (Qother < 3×10^6) attributed by authors to the beta-Ta/Si MS interface, classified here as dielectric_substrate loss. Cross-platform comparison with sapphire substrate results is a primary science focus.

fabrication_details.description: Photoresist: MP S1813 spun at 3000 rpm, baked 1 min at 90°C; patterned using G-line stepper; developed in MP 321 MIF developer for 1 min

fabrication_details.description: Measurement packaging: resonator chips enclosed in Berkeley black-coated copper shim package to absorb stray photons and prevent quasiparticle generation. Used in Meehl, McMahon, McMahon2, and Fatemi fridges.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Whether the deposition chamber has been used for magnetic materials (e.g., Fe, Co, Ni, Py) prior to or concurrently with superconducting film deposition. Categorical: 'superconductor-only', 'magnetic-contaminated-separate-runs', 'magnetic-contaminated-co-loaded'.
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This paper directly tests whether a magnetically contaminated chamber degrades superconducting film quality. The result (no degradation for bulk film, minor surface-only contamination) is highly consequential for community lab resource sharing. Tracking this systematically across the corpus would allow quantitative assessment of contamination risk thresholds.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text abstract and page 2: 'deposition procedure 1 (DP1)' vs 'deposition procedure 2 (DP2)' distinction; SIMS results in Fig. 1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The specific resist strip bath product used after dry etch patterning, as a categorical label (e.g., MICROPOSIT 1165, AZ 300T, NMP, PGMEA). Distinct from the process chemistry field which captures full conditions.
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This paper demonstrates that strip bath identity (1165 vs AZ 300T) is the primary determinant of whether Cl etch residue is removed from the Si substrate-air interface, which in turn determines whether post-HF treatment is necessary to achieve low Qi. This is a reproducible, lab-transferable variable that should be structured for systematic comparison.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 4: 'We find significant differences in device performance and surface chemical content when using the two different resist strip baths'; Table I

catchall.free_notes: This sample (DP1/BOE/1165/no acid) represents the baseline/worst-case condition: 1165 strip without any post-fab acid treatment. LP losses are ~15× higher than the acid-treated 1165 samples. The paper's main finding is that AZ 300T strip bath without HF achieves comparable performance to 1165 with HF, because AZ 300T removes Cl etch residue while 1165 does not.
